title: AI Security, Resilience, and Special ISO/IEC 42001 Considerations
summary: Cover the AI-specific threats, governance resilience measures, and special themes that make AI governance distinct from conventional IT governance.

pages.title: Security threats specific to AI systems

sections.heading: Input manipulation and injection
bullets: Direct injection that explicitly tries to bypass safety controls
Indirect injection hidden in external content that the model reads
Adversarial evasion that adds subtle noise to manipulate classification

sections.heading: Integrity, privacy, and agentic threats
bullets: Data poisoning and model hijacking in the supply chain
Model inversion, membership inference, and model theft
Excessive agency, memory poisoning, and agent-to-agent phishing

images.alt: Matrix of AI security threats covering manipulation, supply chain, privacy, and agentic risks.
images.caption: AI governance must account for prompt injection, model compromise, inference attacks, and autonomous-agent abuse.

pages.callout: Effective AI governance must address both business risk and system-level security risk.

sections.heading: From security to resilience
paragraphs: Security identifies vulnerabilities, while resilience defines how the organization survives, responds, and improves when those vulnerabilities are exploited.
An effective AIMS treats threats through governance, secure development, access controls, monitoring, testing, incident response, and clear accountability.

sections.heading: The human layer
bullets: Eliminate shadow AI by bringing unauthorized employee use into a governed environment.
Teach staff to recognize emerging risks such as agent-to-agent phishing.
Define who is legally and operationally accountable when AI agents make mistakes.

images.alt: Diagram linking shadow AI removal, accountability, operations, and AI literacy to workforce readiness.
images.caption: Resilience depends on both technical controls and a workforce that can govern AI use in practice.

sections.heading: Three integrated pillars
paragraphs: The course introduces privacy, cybersecurity, and legal as an integrated governance trifecta for AI oversight.
ISO/IEC 42001 acts as the operational blueprint that helps those disciplines function as one system rather than as disconnected silos.
bullets: Privacy protects rights through data minimization, purpose limitation, fairness, and transparency.
Cybersecurity protects models, data, and technical architecture against compromise and manipulation.
Legal aligns governance with liability, contractual exposure, and evolving AI regulation.

images.alt: Diagram showing the AI governance trifecta aligned with plan, do, check, and act.
images.caption: The governance trifecta links privacy, cybersecurity, and legal into one operating model for responsible AI.

pages.title: ISO standards and information security alignment

sections.heading: Why ISO/IEC 27001 still matters
paragraphs: Organizations increasingly need to align AI governance with information security rather than treat them as separate agendas.
ISO/IEC 27001 enables an Information Security Management System that protects information assets through risk-based controls, governance, and continual improvement.

sections.heading: How ISO/IEC 42001 complements it
paragraphs: ISO/IEC 42001 establishes an AIMS focused on responsible development, provision, or use of AI systems.
In practice, many organizations need both standards because AI risk intersects with information security, privacy, quality, and operational resilience.

pages.title: Special considerations in ISO/IEC 42001

sections.heading: Themes that make AI governance distinctive
paragraphs: ISO/IEC 42001 reflects the fact that AI systems can create dynamic impacts on individuals, organizations, and society across the full lifecycle.
bullets: Ethics: fairness, human oversight, accountability, and consequences for affected groups
Privacy: disciplined management of data, roles, documentation, and controls
Security: risk treatment across design, data, deployment, and third-party dependencies
Transparency: information, reporting, and documentation that make AI use understandable

images.alt: Diagram highlighting ethics, privacy, security risk identification, and transparency requirements in ISO 42001.
images.caption: ISO/IEC 42001 adds AI-specific considerations around ethics, privacy, security, and transparency.

pages.callout: AI governance goes beyond generic IT control because AI can adapt, scale, and influence human outcomes in less predictable ways.

questions.prompt: Reconstructing sensitive training data (e.g., medical records) through targeted queries is called __________.
questions.explanation: Model inversion refers to inferring sensitive training information from model behavior or outputs.

questions.prompt: ________ theft means replicating a proprietary model's behavior via automated API scraping.
questions.explanation: Model theft or model extraction reproduces model behavior without authorization.

questions.prompt: Which of these is not a component of AI Governance Trifecta?
questions.explanation: The course frames the trifecta as privacy, cybersecurity, and legal.

questions.prompt: ISO _______ allows organizations to develop, monitor, and responsibly use or provide AI-enabled products and services.
questions.explanation: ISO/IEC 42001 is the AIMS standard for responsible AI governance.

questions.prompt: ISO _______ allows organizations to establish an information security management system.
questions.explanation: ISO/IEC 27001 is the management-system standard for information security.

questions.prompt: Bringing unauthorized employee AI use into a governed environment is _____________
questions.explanation: The course uses the phrase eliminating shadow AI for governed control of unofficial AI use.

questions.prompt: ISO/IEC 42001 standard requires organizations to identify and manage risks throughout the AI  _______.
questions.explanation: The standard expects risks to be managed across the full AI system lifecycle.
